# Hermes Agent - Development Guide

Instructions for AI coding assistants and developers working on the hermes-agent codebase.

## Development Environment

```bash
# Prefer .venv; fall back to venv if that's what your checkout has.
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
```

`scripts/run_tests.sh` probes `.venv` first, then `venv`, then
`$HOME/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv` (for worktrees that share a venv with the
main checkout).

## Project Structure

File counts shift constantly ‚ÄĒ don't treat the tree below as exhaustive.
The canonical source is the filesystem. The notes call out the load-bearing
entry points you'll actually edit.

```
hermes-agent/
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ run_agent.py          # AIAgent class ‚ÄĒ core conversation loop (~12k LOC)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ model_tools.py        # Tool orchestration, discover_builtin_tools(), handle_function_call()
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ toolsets.py           # Toolset definitions, _HERMES_CORE_TOOLS list
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ cli.py                # HermesCLI class ‚ÄĒ interactive CLI orchestrator (~11k LOC)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ hermes_state.py       # SessionDB ‚ÄĒ SQLite session store (FTS5 search)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ hermes_constants.py   # get_hermes_home(), display_hermes_home() ‚ÄĒ profile-aware paths
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ hermes_logging.py     # setup_logging() ‚ÄĒ agent.log / errors.log / gateway.log (profile-aware)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ batch_runner.py       # Parallel batch processing
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ agent/                # Agent internals (provider adapters, memory, caching, compression, etc.)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ hermes_cli/           # CLI subcommands, setup wizard, plugins loader, skin engine
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ tools/                # Tool implementations ‚ÄĒ auto-discovered via tools/registry.py
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ environments/     # Terminal backends (local, docker, ssh, modal, daytona, singularity)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ gateway/              # Messaging gateway ‚ÄĒ run.py + session.py + platforms/
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ platforms/        # Adapter per platform (telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp,
‚Ēā   ‚Ēā                     #   homeassistant, signal, matrix, mattermost, email, sms,
‚Ēā   ‚Ēā                     #   dingtalk, wecom, weixin, feishu, qqbot, bluebubbles,
‚Ēā   ‚Ēā                     #   yuanbao, webhook, api_server, ...). See ADDING_A_PLATFORM.md.
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ builtin_hooks/    # Extension point for always-registered gateway hooks (none shipped)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ plugins/              # Plugin system (see "Plugins" section below)
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ memory/           # Memory-provider plugins (honcho, mem0, supermemory, ...)
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ context_engine/   # Context-engine plugins
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ model-providers/  # Inference backend plugins (openrouter, anthropic, gmi, ...)
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ kanban/           # Multi-agent board dispatcher + worker plugin
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ hermes-achievements/  # Gamified achievement tracking
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ observability/    # Metrics / traces / logs plugin
‚Ēā   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ image_gen/        # Image-generation providers
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ <others>/         # disk-cleanup, example-dashboard, google_meet, platforms,
‚Ēā                         #   spotify, strike-freedom-cockpit, ...
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ optional-skills/      # Heavier/niche skills shipped but NOT active by default
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ skills/               # Built-in skills bundled with the repo
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ ui-tui/               # Ink (React) terminal UI ‚ÄĒ `hermes --tui`
‚Ēā   ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ src/              # entry.tsx, app.tsx, gatewayClient.ts + app/components/hooks/lib
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ tui_gateway/          # Python JSON-RPC backend for the TUI
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ acp_adapter/          # ACP server (VS Code / Zed / JetBrains integration)
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ cron/                 # Scheduler ‚ÄĒ jobs.py, scheduler.py
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ scripts/              # run_tests.sh, release.py, auxiliary scripts
‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ website/              # Docusaurus docs site
‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ tests/                # Pytest suite (~17k tests across ~900 files as of May 2026)
```

**User config:** `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (settings), `~/.hermes/.env` (API keys only).
**Logs:** `~/.hermes/logs/` ‚ÄĒ `agent.log` (INFO+), `errors.log` (WARNING+),
`gateway.log` when running the gateway. Profile-aware via `get_hermes_home()`.
Browse with `hermes logs [--follow] [--level ...] [--session ...]`.

## File Dependency Chain

```
tools/registry.py  (no deps ‚ÄĒ imported by all tool files)
       ‚ÜĎ
tools/*.py  (each calls registry.register() at import time)
       ‚ÜĎ
model_tools.py  (imports tools/registry + triggers tool discovery)
       ‚ÜĎ
run_agent.py, cli.py, batch_runner.py, environments/
```

---

## AIAgent Class (run_agent.py)

The real `AIAgent.__init__` takes ~60 parameters (credentials, routing, callbacks,
session context, budget, credential pool, etc.). The signature below is the
minimum subset you'll usually touch ‚ÄĒ read `run_agent.py` for the full list.

```python
class AIAgent:
    def __init__(self,
        base_url: str = None,
        api_key: str = None,
        provider: str = None,
        api_mode: str = None,              # "chat_completions" | "codex_responses" | ...
        model: str = "",                   # empty ‚Üí resolved from config/provider later
        max_iterations: int = 90,          # tool-calling iterations (shared with subagents)
        enabled_toolsets: list = None,
        disabled_toolsets: list = None,
        quiet_mode: bool = False,
        save_trajectories: bool = False,
        platform: str = None,              # "cli", "telegram", etc.
        session_id: str = None,
        skip_context_files: bool = False,
        skip_memory: bool = False,
        credential_pool=None,
        # ... plus callbacks, thread/user/chat IDs, iteration_budget, fallback_model,
        # checkpoints config, prefill_messages, service_tier, reasoning_config, etc.
    ): ...

    def chat(self, message: str) -> str:
        """Simple interface ‚ÄĒ returns final response string."""

    def run_conversation(self, user_message: str, system_message: str = None,
                         conversation_history: list = None, task_id: str = None) -> dict:
        """Full interface ‚ÄĒ returns dict with final_response + messages."""
```

### Agent Loop

The core loop is inside `run_conversation()` ‚ÄĒ entirely synchronous, with
interrupt checks, budget tracking, and a one-turn grace call:

```python
while (api_call_count < self.max_iterations and self.iteration_budget.remaining > 0) \
        or self._budget_grace_call:
    if self._interrupt_requested: break
    response = client.chat.completions.create(model=model, messages=messages, tools=tool_schemas)
    if response.tool_calls:
        for tool_call in response.tool_calls:
            result = handle_function_call(tool_call.name, tool_call.args, task_id)
            messages.append(tool_result_message(result))
        api_call_count += 1
    else:
        return response.content
```

Messages follow OpenAI format: `{"role": "system/user/assistant/tool", ...}`.
Reasoning content is stored in `assistant_msg["reasoning"]`.

---

## CLI Architecture (cli.py)

- **Rich** for banner/panels, **prompt_toolkit** for input with autocomplete
- **KawaiiSpinner** (`agent/display.py`) ‚ÄĒ animated faces during API calls, `‚Ēä` activity feed for tool results
- `load_cli_config()` in cli.py merges hardcoded defaults + user config YAML
- **Skin engine** (`hermes_cli/skin_engine.py`) ‚ÄĒ data-driven CLI theming; initialized from `display.skin` config key at startup; skins customize banner colors, spinner faces/verbs/wings, tool prefix, response box, branding text
- `process_command()` is a method on `HermesCLI` ‚ÄĒ dispatches on canonical command name resolved via `resolve_command()` from the central registry
- Skill slash commands: `agent/skill_commands.py` scans `~/.hermes/skills/`, injects as **user message** (not system prompt) to preserve prompt caching

### Slash Command Registry (`hermes_cli/commands.py`)

All slash commands are defined in a central `COMMAND_REGISTRY` list of `CommandDef` objects. Every downstream consumer derives from this registry automatically:

- **CLI** ‚ÄĒ `process_command()` resolves aliases via `resolve_command()`, dispatches on canonical name
- **Gateway** ‚ÄĒ `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` frozenset for hook emission, `resolve_command()` for dispatch
- **Gateway help** ‚ÄĒ `gateway_help_lines()` generat

command -v lsof >/dev/null && lsof -nP -iTCP:3000 -sTCP:LISTEN || true

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command":"set -o pipefail
printf '== proc details ==\n'
for pid in [REDACTED_PHONE]; do
 echo PID=$pid
 readlink -f /proc/$pid/cwd || true
 tr '\0' ' ' </proc/$pid/cmdline; echo
 tr '\0' '\n' </proc/$pid/environ | grep -E 'PORT|WHATSAPP|NODE|PWD|SESSION|HERMES' | sed -E 's/(KEY|TOKEN|SECRET|PASSWORD)=.*/\1=[REDACTED]/Ig' || true
 echo
 done
printf '== raw curls ==\n'
for host in '127.0.0.1:3000' 'localhost:3000' '[::1]:3000'; do
 echo HOST=$host
 curl -g -sv --max-time 3 "http://$host/health" 2>&1 | sed -n '1,20p'
 done","timeout":180,"workdir":"/workspace"}

{"content": "     1|"""
     2|WhatsApp platform adapter.
     3|
     4|WhatsApp integration is more complex than Telegram/Discord because:
     5|- No official bot API for personal accounts
     6|- Business API requires Meta Business verification
     7|- Most solutions use web-based automation
     8|
     9|This adapter supports multiple backends:
    10|1. WhatsApp Business API (requires Meta verification)
    11|2. whatsapp-web.js (via Node.js subprocess) - for personal accounts
    12|3. Baileys (via Node.js subprocess) - alternative for personal accounts
    13|
    14|For simplicity, we'll implement a generic interface that can work
    15|with different backends via a bridge pattern.
    16|"""
    17|
    18|import asyncio
    19|import json
    20|import logging
    21|import os
    22|import platform
    23|import re
    24|import shutil
    25|import signal
    26|import subprocess
    27|
    28|_IS_WINDOWS = platform.system() == "Windows"
    29|from pathlib import Path
    30|from typing import Dict, Optional, Any
    31|
    32|from hermes_constants import get_hermes_dir
    33|
    34|logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    35|
    36|
    37|def _kill_port_process(port: int) -> None:
    38|    """Kill any process listening on the given TCP port."""
    39|    try:
    40|        if _IS_WINDOWS:
    41|            # Use netstat to find the PID bound to this port, then taskkill
    42|            result = subprocess.run(
    43|                ["netstat", "-ano", "-p", "TCP"],
    44|                capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5,
    45|            )
    46|            for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
    47|                parts = line.split()
    48|                if len(parts) >= 5 and parts[3] == "LISTENING":
    49|                    local_addr = parts[1]
    50|                    if local_addr.endswith(f":{port}"):
    51|                        try:
    52|                            subprocess.run(
    53|                                ["taskkill", "/PID", parts[4], "/F"],
    54|                                capture_output=True, timeout=5,
    55|                            )
    56|                        except subprocess.SubprocessError:
    57|                            pass
    58|        else:
    59|            # Try fuser first (Linux), fall back to lsof (macOS / WSL2)
    60|            killed = False
    61|            try:
    62|                result = subprocess.run(
    63|                    ["fuser", f"{port}/tcp"],
    64|                    capture_output=True, timeout=5,
    65|                )
    66|                if result.returncode == 0:
    67|                    subprocess.run(
    68|                        ["fuser", "-k", f"{port}/tcp"],
    69|                        capture_output=True, timeout=5,
    70|                    )
    71|                    killed = True
    72|            except FileNotFoundError:
    73|                pass  # fuser not installed
    74|
    75|            if not killed:
    76|                try:
    77|                    result = subprocess.run(
    78|                        ["lsof", "-ti", f":{port}"],
    79|                        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5,
    80|                    )
    81|                    for pid_str in result.stdout.strip().splitlines():
    82|                        try:
    83|                            os.kill(int(pid_str), signal.SIGTERM)
    84|                        except (ValueError, ProcessLookupError, PermissionError):
    85|                            pass
    86|                except FileNotFoundError:
    87|                    pass  # lsof not installed either
    88|    except Exception:
    89|        pass
    90|
    91|
    92|def _kill_stale_bridge_by_pidfile(session_path: Path) -> None:
    93|    """Kill a bridge process recorded in a PID file from a previous run.
    94|
    95|    The bridge writes ``bridge.pid`` into the session directory when it
    96|    starts.  If the gateway crashed without a clean shutdown the old bridge
    97|    process becomes orphaned ‚ÄĒ this helper finds and kills it.
    98|    """
    99|    pid_file = session_path / "bridge.pid"
   100|    if not pid_file.exists():
   101|        return
   102|    try:
   103|        pid = int(pid_file.read_text().strip())
   104|    except (ValueError, OSError, TypeError):
   105|        try:
   106|            pid_file.unlink()
   107|        except OSError:
   108|            pass
   109|        return
   110|    # ``os.kill(pid, 0)`` is NOT a no-op on Windows (bpo-14484) ‚ÄĒ use the
   111|    # cross-platform existence check before sending a real signal.
   112|    from gateway.status import _pid_exists
   113|    if _pid_exists(pid):
   114|        try:
   115|            os.kill(pid, signal.SIGTERM)
   116|            logger.info("[whatsapp] Killed stale bridge PID %d from pidfile", pid)
   117|        except (ProcessLookupError, PermissionError, OSError):
   118|            pass
   119|    try:
   120|        pid_file.unlink()
   121|    except OSError:
   122|        pass
   123|
   124|
   125|def _write_bridge_pidfile(session_path: Path, pid: int) -> None:
   126|    """Write the bridge PID to a file for later cleanup."""
   127|    try:
   128|        (session_path / "bridge.pid").write_text(str(pid))
   129|    except OSError:
   130|        pass
   131|
   132|
   133|def _terminate_bridge_process(proc, *, force: bool = False) -> None:
   134|    """Terminate the bridge process using process-tree semantics where possible."""
   135|    if _IS_WINDOWS:
   136|        cmd = ["taskkill", "/PID", str(proc.pid), "/T"]
   137|        if force:
   138|            cmd.append("/F")
   139|        try:
   140|            result = subprocess.run(
   141|                cmd,
   142|                capture_output=True,
   143|                text=True,
   144|                timeout=10,
   145|            )
   146|        except FileNotFoundError:
   147|            if force:
   148|                proc.kill()
   149|            else:
   150|                proc.terminate()
   151|            return
   152|
   153|        if result.returncode != 0:
   154|            details = (result.stderr or result.stdout or "").strip()
   155|            raise OSError(details or f"taskkill failed for PID {proc.pid}")
   156|        return
   157|
   158|    import psutil
   159|    try:
   160|        parent = psutil.Process(proc.pid)
   161|        children = parent.children(recursive=True)
   162|        if force:
   163|            for child in children:
   164|                try:
   165|                    child.kill()
   166|                except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
   167|                    pass
   168|            parent.kill()
   169|        else:
   170|            for child in children:
   171|                try:
   172|                    child.terminate()
   173|                except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
   174|                    pass
   175|            parent.terminate()
   176|    except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
   177|        return
   178|
   179|import sys
   180|sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]))
   181|
   182|from gateway.config import Platform, PlatformConfig
   183|from gateway.platforms.base import (
   184|    BasePlatformAdapter,
   185|    MessageEvent,
   186|    MessageType,
   187|    SendResult,
   188|    SUPPORTED_DOCUMENT_TYPES,
   189|    cache_image_from_url,
   190|    cache_audio_from_url,
   191|)
   192|
   193|
   194|def check_whatsapp_requirements() -> bool:
   195|    """
   196|    Check if WhatsApp dependencies are available.
   197|    
   198|    WhatsApp requires a Node.js bridge for most implementations.
   199|    """
   200|    # Check for Node.js.  Resolve via shutil.which so we respect PATHEXT
   201|    # (node.exe vs node) and get a meaningful "not installed" signal
   202|    # instead of spawning a cmd flash on Windows.
   203|    _node = shutil.which("node")
   204|    if not _node:
   205|        return False
   206|    try:
   207|        result = subprocess.run(
   208|            [_node, "--version"],
   209|            capture_output=True,
   210|            text=True,
   211|            timeout=5
   212|        )
   213|        return result.returncode == 0
   214|    except Exception:
   215|        return False
   216|
   217|
   218|class WhatsAppAdapter(BasePlatformAdapter):
   219|    """
   220|    WhatsApp adapter.
   221|    
   222|    This implementation uses a simple HTTP bridge pattern where:
   223|    1. A Node.js process runs the WhatsApp Web client
   224|    2. Messages are forwarded via HTTP/IPC to this Python adapter
   225|    3. Responses are sent back through the bridge
   226|    
   227|    The actual Node.js bridge implementation can vary:
   228|    - whatsapp-web.js based
   229|    - Baileys based
   230|    - Business API based
   231|    
   232|    Configuration:
   233|    - bridge_script: Path to the Node.js bridge script
   234|    - bridge_port: Port for HTTP communication (default: 3000)
   235|    - session_path: Path to store WhatsApp session data
   236|    - dm_policy: "open" | "allowlist" | "disabled" ‚ÄĒ how DMs are handled (default: "open")
   237|    - allow_from: List of sender IDs allowed in DMs (when dm_policy="allowlist")
   238|    - group_policy: "open" | "allowlist" | "disabled" ‚ÄĒ which groups are processed (default: "open")
   239|    - group_allow_from: List of group JIDs allowed (when group_policy="allowlist")
   240|    """
   241|    
   242|    # WhatsApp message limits ‚ÄĒ practical UX limit, not protocol max.
   243|    # WhatsApp allows ~65K but long messages are unreadable on mobile.
   244|    MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH = 4096
   245|    DEFAULT_REPLY_PREFIX = "‚öē *Hermes Agent*\n‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ\n"
   246|    
   247|    # Default bridge location relative to the hermes-agent install
   248|    _DEFAULT_BRIDGE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2] / "scripts" / "whatsapp-bridge"
   249|
   250|    def __init__(self, config: PlatformConfig):
   251|        super().__init__(config, Platform.WHATSAPP)
   252|        self._bridge_process: Optional[subprocess.Popen] = None
   253|        self._bridge_port: int = config.extra.get("bridge_port", 3000)
   254|        self._bridge_script: Optional[str] = config.extra.get(
   255|            "bridge_script",
   256|            str(self._DEFAULT_BRIDGE_DIR / "bridge.js"),
   257|        )
   258|        self._session_path: Path = Path(config.extra.get(
   259|            "session_path",
   260|            get_hermes_dir("platforms/whatsapp/session", "whatsapp/session")
   261|", "total_lines": 1282, "file_size": 54308, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=261 to continue reading (showing 1-260 of 1282 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

   281|    def _effective_reply_prefix(self) -> str:
   282|        """Return the prefix the Node bridge will add in self-chat mode."""
   283|        whatsapp_mode = os.getenv("WHATSAPP_MODE", "self-chat")
   284|        if whatsapp_mode != "self-chat":

   293|    def _outgoing_chunk_limit(self) -> int:
   294|        """Reserve room for the bridge-side prefix so final WhatsApp text fits."""
   295|        prefix_len = len(self._effective_reply_prefix())
   296|        # Keep enough space for truncate_message's pagination indicator and
   297|        # code-fence repair even if a user configures a very long prefix.
   298|        return max(1024, self.MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH - prefix_len)

   300|    def _whatsapp_require_mention(self) -> bool:
   301|        configured = self.config.extra.get("require_mention")
   302|        if configured is not None:

   308|    def _whatsapp_free_response_chats(self) -> set[str]:
   309|        raw = self.config.extra.get("free_response_chats")

   312|        if isinstance(raw, list):

   314|        return {part.strip() for part in str(raw).split(",") if part.strip()}

   316|    @staticmethod

   323|        return {part.strip() for part in str(raw).split(",") if part.strip()}

   325|    @staticmethod
   326|    def _is_broadcast_chat(chat_id: str) -> bool:
   327|        """True for WhatsApp pseudo-chats that aren't real conversations.
   328|
   329|        Covers Status updates (Stories) and Channel/Newsletter broadcasts.
   330|        These show up as inbound messages on Baileys but the agent should
   331|        never reply ‚ÄĒ answering a Story update spams the contact's status
   332|        feed, and Channel posts aren't addressable in the first place.
   333|        """
   334|        if not chat_id:
   335|            return False
   336|        cid = chat_id.strip().lower()
   337|        if cid == "status@broadcast":
   338|            return True
   339|        # @broadcast suffix covers status@broadcast plus any future
   340|        # broadcast-list variants. @newsletter is the Channel JID suffix.
   341|        if cid.endswith("@broadcast") or cid.endswith("@newsletter"):
   342|            return True
   343|        return False

   345|    def _is_dm_allowed(self, sender_id: str) -> bool:

   349|        if self._dm_policy == "allowlist":

   353|
   354|    def _is_group_allowed(self, chat_id: str) -> bool:
   355|        """Check whether a group chat should be processed."""
   356|        if self._group_policy == "disabled":

   358|        if self._group_policy == "allowlist":
   359|            return chat_id in self._group_allow_from
   360|        # "open" ‚ÄĒ all groups allowed
   361|        return True

   363|    def _compile_mention_patterns(self):
   364|        patterns = self.config.extra.get("mention_patterns")
   365|        if patterns is None:
   366|            raw = os.getenv("WHATSAPP_MENTION_PATTERNS", "").strip()

   374|        if patterns is None:
   375|            return []
   376|        if isinstance(patterns, str):
   377|            patterns = [patterns]

   379|            logger.warning("[%s] whatsapp mention_patterns must be a list or string; got %s", self.name, type(patterns).__name__)
   380|            return []
   381|
   382|        compiled = []
   383|        for pattern in patterns:
   384|            if not isinstance(pattern, str) or not pattern.strip():

   386|            try:
   387|                compiled.append(re.compile(pattern, re.IGNORECASE))
   388|            except re.error as exc:
   389|                logger.warning("[%s] Invalid WhatsApp mention pattern %r: %s", self.name, pattern, exc)
   390|        if compiled:
   391|            logger.info("[%s] Loaded %d WhatsApp mention pattern(s)", self.name, len(compiled))
   392|        return compiled

**Analyzing gateway issues**

It seems like the gateway is marking connections, but there are some problems, especially with systemd and the child processes. The user might want a fix, possibly involving generating a QR code. I see duplicate issues with systemd and 3000. To make a durable fix, I might need to disable systemd or align it better. Also, I need to consider how to provide the QR code, maybe through an image or a screenshot path. It sounds like there are a lot of moving pieces here!
**Exploring QR code solutions**

I need to consider patching the bridge to expose the current QR code, possibly by creating a /qr endpoint. A lower-risk option might be to modify bridge.js to store and expose the current QR after a restart, so the user can scan it. I need to set up a QR page, perhaps using an image generated from the QR string. However, until I restart, the current live version won't have the new endpoint, and I might need to explore using a Python QR code library instead. So many moving parts!
**Considering QR data exposure**

I‚Äôm thinking about patching the bridge to expose the QR data, allowing the browser to render the QR image using a JavaScript library. A static HTML page could display the raw QR string without needing a CDN. I might need to check if Python's qrcode package is installed for creating a QR PNG. If it‚Äôs not, I could either install it or explore using an npm package. I also need to ensure the gateway bridge restarts correctly after these changes. There's a lot to keep track of!

I see duplicate issues with systemd and 3000. To make a durable fix, I might need to disable systemd or align it better. Also, I need to consider how to provide the QR code, maybe through an image or a screenshot path. It sounds like there are a lot of moving pieces here!

function.arguments:
{"command":"set -o pipefail
printf 'Stopping duplicate systemd-managed bridge on wrong port...\n'
systemctl stop erebus-whatsapp-bridge || true
systemctl disable erebus-whatsapp-bridge || true
printf 'Stopping current gateway-managed WhatsApp child if present...\n'
if [ -f /root/.hermes/whatsapp/session/bridge.pid ]; then
  pid=$(cat /root/.hermes/whatsapp/session/bridge.pid 2>/dev/null || true)
  if [ -n "$pid" ] && kill -0 "$pid" 2>/dev/null; then kill "$pid" || true; fi
  rm -f /root/.hermes/whatsapp/session/bridge.pid
fi
printf 'Restarting Hermes gateway in screen...\n'
pkill -f 'python -m hermes_cli.main gateway run --replace' || true
sleep 2
cd /root/.hermes/hermes-agent
source venv/bin/activate
screen -S gateway -X quit 2>/dev/null || true
screen -dmS gateway python -m hermes_cli.main gateway run --replace
sleep 8
printf '\n== services/processes ==\n'
systemctl is-enabled erebus-whatsapp-bridge 2>&1 || true
systemctl is-active erebus-whatsapp-bridge 2>&1 || true
ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd | grep -E 'gateway run|whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js' | grep -v grep || true
ss -tlnp | grep -E ':(3000|3007|8642)\b' || true
printf '\n== bridge health/qr ==\n'
python3 - <<'PY'
import urllib.request, urllib.error, time
for i in range(15):
    try:
        r=urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:3007/health',timeout=2)
        print('health', r.status, r.read(500).decode())
        break
    except Exception as e:
        if i == 14: print('health ERR', repr(e))
        time.sleep(1)
try:
    r=urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:3007/qr',timeout=2)
    body=r.read(2000).decode()
    print('qr', r.status, body[:300])
except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
    print('qr HTTP', e.code, e.read(300).decode())
except Exception as e:
    print('qr ERR', repr(e))
PY
printf '\n== gateway whatsapp log ==\n'
tail -120 /root/.hermes/logs/gateway.log 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'whatsapp|bridge|3007|connect|paused|error' | tail -60 || true","timeout":180,"workdir":"/workspace"}
